title: Files and exceptions
summary: Read and write files safely, and handle failures in ways that preserve signal instead of hiding bugs.

items.prompt: Why is `with open(...)` usually better than `open(...)` alone?

solution.summary: It guarantees that the file is cleaned up promptly, even if an error occurs while you are working with it.

hints: Use exceptions when failure is possible and expected.
items.prompt: Which situation is the best use of a `try`/`except` block?

choices.label: Handling a file that might not exist when the user provides the path.
Replacing every `if` statement in your codebase.

solution.summary: File access can fail for reasons outside your control, so catching the specific error lets you respond cleanly.

items.prompt: Why should you catch specific exceptions instead of using a bare `except`?

solution.summary: Specific exceptions preserve useful failures and make it much easier to understand what actually went wrong.

items.prompt: Write a function `safe_divide(a, b)` that returns `a / b`, but returns the string `'cannot divide by zero'` when `b` is zero.

solution.summary: Use the reference solution to compare structure, not just syntax.
